questions.question: Why does every project get its own virtual environment (.venv)?
options: So each project's package versions stay isolated and don't conflict
Because pip refuses to work without one
questions.explanation: A venv isolates dependencies per project — Project A can use pandas 2.x while Project B stays on 1.x without conflicts.

questions.question: What does 'selecting an interpreter' in VS Code actually choose?
options: Which Python installation/venv runs and analyzes your code
The color theme of the editor
Which git branch you are on
questions.explanation: The interpreter setting tells VS Code which Python (system or a specific .venv) to use for running, debugging and IntelliSense.

questions.question: What does a breakpoint let you do?
options: Pause the program at a line and inspect variable values
Delete a line of code permanently
questions.explanation: Debugging with breakpoints pauses execution so you can step through code and inspect state — far more powerful than print statements.

questions.question: Your terminal prompt shows (.venv) at the start. What does that indicate?
options: The virtual environment is activated — pip installs go into it
You are in the root directory
questions.explanation: The (.venv) prefix means the environment is active, so python and pip resolve to the project's isolated environment.

questions.question: What does Pylance add to VS Code?
options: IntelliSense: autocomplete, type checking and code navigation for Python
questions.explanation: Pylance is the language server installed with the Python extension — it powers completions, hover docs and type checking.

questions.question: What is Ollama?
options: A tool that runs open LLMs locally and serves them via an API
questions.explanation: Ollama downloads open models (Llama, Qwen, Mistral…) and runs them on your machine, exposing an API at localhost:11434.

questions.question: Why did you pick the model size (3b vs 7b vs 14b) based on your RAM?
options: Model weights must fit in memory — too big a model won't run or will be extremely slow
Bigger models cost more money per query
RAM only matters for the frontend
questions.explanation: A model's parameters load into RAM. A 7B model wants ~16 GB total; on an 8 GB laptop the 3B variant is the practical choice.

questions.question: What is the biggest advantage of a local model for this program?
options: Free and private: no API key, no per-token cost, works offline
It is always smarter than cloud models
questions.explanation: Local models cost nothing to run, keep data on your machine, and work without internet — ideal for training and synthetic-data projects.

questions.question: VS Code's Copilot/Cline talks to your local model through…
options: The HTTP API Ollama serves on localhost:11434
questions.explanation: The editor extensions call Ollama's local HTTP API — the same API your Python code will call later in the program.

questions.question: Later in the program, your chatbot's LLM calls can stay free by…
options: Pointing the openai SDK's base_url at Ollama (local) or Groq's free tier
questions.explanation: Both Ollama and Groq are drop-in compatible with the openai Python SDK — you just change base_url and key, so every exercise works free.

questions.question: Why does chatbot.py append both the user message AND the model's reply to history?
options: The model is stateless — it only 'remembers' what you resend in the messages list each call
questions.explanation: LLM chat APIs have no memory between calls. Resending the running history is what makes follow-up questions work.

questions.question: What is the difference between git commit and git push?
options: Commit records a snapshot locally; push uploads your commits to GitHub
questions.explanation: Commits are local history checkpoints. Push publishes them to the remote — which is what our verification and recruiters can see.

questions.question: Why does the backend expose a /health endpoint from day one?
options: Monitoring, Docker health checks and Kubernetes probes will all hit it later in the program
questions.explanation: A trivial /health route is the standard liveness signal — Days 28–29 wire Docker HEALTHCHECK and k8s probes to this exact endpoint.

questions.question: What belongs in .gitignore?
options: Generated/local files like .venv/, __pycache__/ and secrets like .env
questions.explanation: Environments, caches and secrets are machine-local or sensitive; committing them bloats the repo and can leak keys.

questions.question: What does requirements.txt do?
options: Lists your project's dependencies so anyone can recreate the environment with pip install -r
questions.explanation: requirements.txt pins what your project needs — Docker builds in week 4 install from this exact file.
